channel.title: TTRPG on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in TTRPG on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Traditional Game Mechanics: What I Tried
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about traditional game mechanics and what I tried to do in video games based on those mechanics.

This comes from a question from sherner, who asked: I know You’ made a video game about board games that have influenced you, but I was wondering, since you mentioned reactivity, multiple solution puzzles and roleplay as defining elements of your games. As you said it yourself, all stem back to traditional ttrpg design choices, obviously D and D being our biggest reference. So what other elements of traditional gaming, IE non-digital formats such as board games, have influenced Your Design philosophy and style? Was it a conscious decision to implement them into your games or would you try to distance video games from traditional gaming? Finally, what other elements have you liked to experiment and transposed video games throughout your career, but you couldn’t figure out a way to do it?

item.title: Adapting TTRPGs vs. Other IPs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adapting tabletop role playing games into video games versus adapting a non-game IP into a video game.

I think the into the video game part should probably be implicit, because I’ve never adapted anything into anything else but a video game. So let’s talk about the pros and cons of having a tabletop RPG versus. Is a non-game RPG a non-game IP? This is an answer to a question from Dr John wubber, who asked: I’m curious, since you did adaptations of d and d and vampire, the Masquerade, but also South Park, what kinds of challenges did you find making a game based on another type of game versus making a game based on something non — interactive like a TV show? That is a really interesting question and no one has ever asked that before. It’s, by the way, that’s if you’re wondering, like what you can do. That’s a John’s question — is really interesting to me because it’s like: oh, I’ve literally done a video.

item.title: Writing My Own TTRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing my own TBL toop roleplaying game.

I’m actually answering a question from all Lucky Seven, who asked: would you like to write a pen and paper RPG? If so, considering your knowledge of computer game design, how would you approach it? What do you think you would make differently from someone who’s never done a crpg?

That’s an interesting question I’ve had. I’ve seen other people ask me like, hey, why don’t you make a tabletop RPG or make a pen and paper RPG? And I’m always like, eh, but this question made me think, oh yeah, making computer RPGs is kind of a different design space than tabletop, and that is an interesting question to me. So let me answer the base question right off the bat, which is, yes, I have thought off and on, but never seriously, about making a tabletop RPG. I think that, having made a bunch of computer RPGs and they’re different, and I’ll talk about that — I think I would have, based on that experience, quite a number of advantages and a few disadvantages if I tried to make a pen and P, pen and pencil, paper and pencil RPG.

item.title: Shadowdark
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Shadow dark.

It is the new thing I’m into. It’s Friday. I figured I’d do a fun Friday about it. Let me tell you why I like it so much.

First of all, there’s been this osr trend. I think it stands for old school rules, but people argue what it stands for. I don’t care.

It’s things like Dungeon Crawl Classics, which I do have a few of. But what I love about o the osr trend is it’s starting to feel like people are looking towards the old days of how tabletop RPGs, RPGs used to be. And for everyone there’s a different reason. Some people like the rules, some people like the settings, some people like, um, just some of the more, some of the Simplicity of it. For whatever reason, I like it CU. For me it’s very nostalgic and when I was looking into osr, I mean I I got the Dungeon Crawl Classics right away. But looking into it, led me to Shadow dark.

item.title: D&D Influences (By Edition)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about DND influences by addition.

Mostly this is influences on me and my work, but in one case it’s going to be my work influencing back into D and D, which I think is really cool. Anyway, this is response to Y l333 who says: hey, Tim, I don’t know about others, but I would love to hear about which editions of D and you’ve played, especially in the TSR days, what D and D books you own and which additions are your favorite and have had the most influence on your games. I’ve done a lot of talking about D and D here and there and I’ve done D and D stories and all that I will talk about, and I’ve talked about influences, tabletop influences, of non D andd stuff. So let’s just talk about Dungeons and Dragons. I have mentioned several times that the way I got introduced to Dungeons and Dragons was a little unusual.

item.title: GURPS Everything
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gerps everything, which was an attempt of mine to run a gerps campaign in the 9s using the basic set and every single Source book that I owned — not the World Books, not the ones that had particular settings in them, but all the source books, like the extensions on, like magic and sanics and I’ll talk about that.

item.title: Dice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about dice.

You see, I did a video, well, from your point of view, I did a px video a few weeks ago. From my point of view, I did it a few days ago because when I did the PX 2024 video, which I’ll link below, I moved it to the front of the queue because pxs have just happened and I don’t do topical stuff very often. So it was like, well, let’s put it in the front of the queue. This video isn’t topical, so I’m going to the end of the queue. So, even though it’s only been a few days for me, it’s weeks for you, which should give you an idea of how big my queue is. I got a lot of requests on that video to talk about my dice because, I mentioned, I have a lot of dice. I have a bit of an addiction to dice. I’ve been ever since I got my first polyhedral set 45 years ago.

item.title: An "Off The Rails" TTRPG Campaign
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about an off the rails tabletop RPG campaign.

This is me answering a question from Wes 9353, who asked: how far off the rail have you gotten a campaign to go? Now let me clarify: this wasn’t my campaign. Uh, someone else was being the GM in this and but I was a player. There were three players: me, Dave and John. I’m not going to tell you what the module was. It was a professional module purchased with money made by a professional game designer for a professionally produced tabletop role playing game system. All I’m going to tell you was superheroes. Good luck finding out which one.

item.title: Using Geometry To Constrain Dice Rolls
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about using geometry to constrain dice rolls.

First, before I get into that, because there’s a history and there’s a video and let’s do a thought experiment. Let’s say you’re making a new tabletop role playing game and you just want to use six-sided dice. And let’s say there’s a skill that requires you to roll two six-sided dice for that skill, you know generating two to 12. But you want it to be more interesting than that. You want something special to happen if you roll a one and you want something good to happen if you roll a six. And then you want something special to happen if you roll a one and a six. So looking at that, you’re like, wait a minute, I don’t want double bads if I roll two ones and I don’t want double Goods if I roll two sixes.

item.title: My AD&D Mods
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about modding the original advanced Dungeons and Dragons.

But before I go into that, let me just say that a lot of people ask me about modding and what I think of current mods and mods for different games. I love them. I’ve said I loved them.

I’m not going to review them, but I’ve been playing and enjoying mods since probably back in the 90s with quake and as recently as Fallout 4. The last time I played it I did a playthrough and then, after I finished the main story arc, I loaded it a bunch of mods just to see what people were doing with that game. And also, when I tell people, if you’re thinking about getting a job in the game industry and you should make a demo, modding an existing game is perfectly okay. If you have an idea for a cool new feature and you can take an existing game and just mod that feature in just to show off how it. Work and how it makes the game and you can demonstrate, like, how it makes the game better, perfect. We hired someone for The Outer Worlds based on a mod he made for Skyrim. So it happens people pay attention to that stuff and I can tell you that that guy’s mod, you know, moved his resume to the top of the pile. So if you’re thinking about modding, do it.

item.title: Tabletop RPG Influences
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about tabletop RPG influences that directly show up in my own computer games.

Specifically, I want to talk about experiences I had either as a DM or as a player that resulted in something going in my game. In fact, I’m going to answer the question from mud MW 4321, who asks: hey, Tim, I remember you talking about your super luck character from gerps, which made you want to put luck into Fallout. Is there anything else in your crpgs that was heavily influenced by your experience in tabletop games? This also goes for narratives that got told at the table and then put into your game.

item.title: Appendix N
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about appendix, and Josh James 582, asked for my thoughts on appendix n, which is in the DM’s guide for advanced Dungeons and Dragons, which lets me show off something I’m super excited about.

I got Gary gygax to sign this for me at a convention in Los Angeles in 92. And later, when I made Temple volento evil, I asked him if he remembered. He was sweet and said he did. I don’t think he really did, but he then said you mentioned Bard’s Tale Construction Set, and I was like maybe he did remember.

item.title: Judges Guild
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim.

As you can see, I got new equipment. My husband gave me an early birthday present of a Sony zv1, so I’m experimenting with a new video, using that as my audio and video Source. Also, I found a shirt. It was packed away from all my other shirts, probably because I got it I don’t know, maybe about six years ago, judges Guild made me think I should talk about Judges Guild, probably the biggest influence to the games I’ve made that nobody ever knows about, nobody ever talks about. So let me explain what they are.
